title: forward_list
description: An even lighter linked list: singly linked, forward-only, push_front() only — and the genuine surprise that it has no size().
objectives: Understand forward_list as a singly linked list
Know it only supports push_front(), not push_back()
Recognize the surprising absence of size()
Understand the trade-off: less memory per node, less flexibility

sections.text: list stores, for every element, a link to both its next AND previous neighbor. forward_list, from the <forward_list> header, stores only a link to the next one — lighter, but with real capability trade-offs.
forward_list is a singly linked list — each element links only to the next one, with no way to go backward, minimizing memory overhead per element.

Unlike list, forward_list has no push_back() at all. Since each node only knows its next neighbor, reaching the very end to append something would require walking the entire list first — so the STL simply doesn't offer that operation, keeping the container honest about what's actually cheap.

forward_list also has no size() member function — a real surprise coming from vector, array, and list, which all have one. This is a deliberate design choice: computing the size would require walking the entire list, and the container's authors chose not to offer a method that looks cheap but secretly isn't.
Each push_front() places its value at the very front, so the three calls (30, then 20, then 10) end up ordered 10, 20, 30 — the most recently pushed value is always first.
Its iterators only support moving forward (it++) — there's no it-- the way a list iterator allows, since there's no backward link to follow. This is exactly what "forward" in the name signals.

forward_list is a niche, specialized choice — useful when memory per element genuinely matters (many small elements, one link instead of two) and only forward traversal and front insertion are ever needed. For general-purpose use, list or vector are far more common defaults.

sections.title: A one-way treasure hunt with no headcount
sections.text: Recall list's treasure hunt analogy. forward_list is the same hunt, but each clue only ever points forward, never back — and nobody along the way is keeping a running count of how many clues exist, since counting them all would mean walking the entire hunt from the start.

sections.title: Common Beginner Mistakes
items: Calling push_back() on a forward_list — it doesn't exist, only push_front() does.
Calling size() on a forward_list — also doesn't exist; counting manually with a loop is the alternative if truly needed.
Trying to iterate backward — forward_list iterators only move forward.
Reaching for forward_list by default — it's a specialized, memory-conscious choice; list or vector are the more common general-purpose defaults.

sections.title: Interview Questions

questions.question: What is the difference between list and forward_list?
questions.answer: list is doubly linked, storing both next and previous links, and supports bidirectional traversal; forward_list is singly linked, storing only a next link, and only supports forward traversal, saving memory per element.

questions.question: Why does forward_list not have push_back()?
questions.answer: Because reaching the end would require walking the entire list from the front, which wouldn't be cheap — the STL deliberately omits operations that would silently be expensive.

questions.question: Why does forward_list not have size()?
questions.answer: Computing it would require traversing the whole list, so it's deliberately left out to avoid offering an operation that looks cheap but actually isn't.

sections.thirtySecondAnswer: forward_list is a singly linked list, storing only a link to each element's next neighbor, unlike list's doubly linked structure. This makes it lighter on memory but more limited: it only supports push_front(), not push_back(), and has no size() at all — both omissions are deliberate, since either operation would secretly require walking the entire list. Its iterators only move forward, matching its name, and it's a specialized choice for cases where minimizing per-element memory matters more than the flexibility list or vector provide.

sections.title: Trick to Remember forward_list
sections.text: forward_list = a one-way treasure hunt, no headcount kept. Only push_front(). No push_back(). No size(). Forward only, no looking back.
